1.matchText: On paper, in 1870, a Black man in Mississippi was a citizen with the vote and the equal protection of the law
1.blurb: This was the achievement of the first Reconstruction's Second Founding. The Fourteenth Amendment of 1868 wrote birthright citizenship and equal protection into the Constitution, and the Reconstruction Acts and Fifteenth Amendment put a ballot in the hands of Black men across the South. For a few years it was partly real: biracial legislatures, Black congressmen, schools built from nothing. The Reconstruction narrative tells how that democracy was constructed and what it took.

Reconstruction did not lapse from neglect. White paramilitaries, the Klan, the White League, the Red Shirts, murdered Black voters and officeholders off the rolls while the North decided enforcement was no longer worth the cost. In the disputed 1876 election the parties cut the Compromise of 1877: the last federal troops came out of the South and enforcement of the amendments simply stopped. The Reconstruction narrative ends on that betrayal, which is the wound this entire book is about.

1.matchText: the deliberate, systematic construction of something new
1.blurb: Between 1877 and 1900 the white South built Jim Crow brick by brick: segregation statutes, disfranchisement by poll tax and literacy test, and lynching as the enforcement arm. The Roaring Twenties narrative opens with that same construction as the wreckage behind the decade, and it tracks the Great Migration this chapter describes, the movement of Black Southerners out of the Jim Crow South and into the Northern cities the civil rights movement would later mobilize.

The Roaring Twenties narrative covers the first Great Migration and the Harlem Renaissance in full: the World War I-era exodus north, the New Negro, Garvey and the UNIA, jazz, and the concentration of Black voters in big Northern industrial states. That concentration is exactly the political leverage A. Philip Randolph would use in 1941, and the runway this chapter begins on.

2.matchText: the same claim that a state could nullify federal law had been the dress rehearsal for secession
2.blurb: The Antebellum America narrative covers the Nullification Crisis of 1832, when South Carolina under John C. Calhoun declared it could void a federal tariff within its borders and Andrew Jackson threatened force. That doctrine, interposition, was the dress rehearsal for secession, and the Southern Manifesto deliberately revived its language to resist Brown. The movement's lawyers were re-litigating a war the Constitution had already won twice.

3.matchText: the enforcement arm of the whole system
3.blurb: The Antebellum America narrative traces the lived reality of slavery and the violence that enforced it. After emancipation that violence did not end; it mutated into the lynching regime, the public, often festive, extralegal murder that policed the color line for the Jim Crow decades. Till's killing was that system working exactly as designed.

4.blurb: Organized Black resistance to slavery, the Underground Railroad, Walker's Appeal, the abolition movement, is the subject of the Antebellum America narrative's resistance chapter. The Montgomery boycott belongs in that lineage: a disciplined, collective, economic refusal, this time wielding the bus company's own dependence on Black riders as leverage rather than appealing to the conscience of the enslaver.

5.matchText: the same lesson the Reconstruction narrative draws from the 1871 federal prosecutions that broke the first Klan
5.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative's Klan Wars chapter is the precedent this passage points to. In 1871-72 the federal government used the Enforcement Acts, suspended habeas corpus, and prosecuted the Klan in South Carolina, and the terror collapsed fast. It proved the night-rider violence was never an unstoppable force of nature; the federal government simply had to choose to bring its power to bear. Little Rock in 1957 was that same lesson, the literal resumption of the enforcement abandoned in 1877.

5.matchText: the same supremacy question raised in the 1830s
5.blurb: When Faubus used the Arkansas Guard to defy a federal court, he raised the supremacy question the Antebellum America narrative covers in the Nullification Crisis of 1832: can a state interpose its sovereignty against federal authority? Calhoun said yes; Jackson threatened force; the Civil War settled it for good. Eisenhower could not concede that a governor could nullify the Constitution without unmaking the answer the war had written.

8.blurb: Barnett's defiance at Ole Miss, like Faubus at Little Rock, was the old nullification claim again: that a state could interpose its sovereignty against a federal court. The Antebellum America narrative covers where that doctrine came from, the Nullification Crisis of 1832, when South Carolina under Calhoun tried it against a tariff and Jackson threatened force. It had already lost the largest possible test in the Civil War, which made every Southern governor who revived it in the 1950s and 1960s re-litigating a settled war.

9.matchText: Thaddeus Stevens's defeated proposal to give the freedpeople land
9.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative devotes a full chapter to why land redistribution failed: Thaddeus Stevens's proposal to break up the plantations and give the freedpeople forty acres was defeated, chiefly by Northern Republicans unwilling to set the precedent of seizing property. The freedpeople got sharecropping and the crop-lien trap instead. The economic half of the March on Washington, Jobs beside Freedom, is that same unmet promise restated nearly a century later.

9.matchText: the coalition behind the Fifteenth Amendment chose to secure the vote for Black men and to defer, indefinitely, the vote for women
9.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative covers the women's-suffrage split: the coalition behind the Fifteenth Amendment chose to secure the ballot for Black men and to defer the vote for women, fracturing the suffrage movement on exactly that decision. A century later the March on Washington's program rationed visibility the same way, seating the women who built the movement and giving none of them the microphone.

10.matchText: an 1883 Supreme Court ruling, the Civil Rights Cases, had held that the Fourteenth Amendment restrained only
10.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative traces how the Supreme Court disarmed the constitutional amendments, the Civil Rights Cases of 1883 holding that the Fourteenth Amendment reached only governments, not private businesses. That ruling was the loophole a segregated motel would invoke eighty years later, which is why the 1964 Congress grounded the act in the Commerce Clause instead and finally shut the private-business door the South had counted on since 1883.

11.matchText: the Reconstruction-era civil rights conspiracy law, the crime of conspiring to deprive someone of their constitutional rights
11.blurb: The conspiracy statute used in the 1967 Mississippi Burning trial was a Reconstruction-era weapon, written into the Enforcement Acts the Reconstruction narrative's Klan Wars chapter covers, the same laws that broke the first Klan in 1871. Ninety-six years later there was still no federal murder statute reaching a private killing, so the only charge the government could bring against Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner's killers was conspiracy to deprive them of their constitutional rights.

12.matchText: named for Edmund Pettus, a Confederate general who became a Reconstruction-era Ku Klux Klan grand dragon in Alabama
12.blurb: Edmund Pettus is the continuity this bridge is named for. The Reconstruction narrative documents how the men who led the 1870s paramilitary terror were not exiled for it but honored and returned to power: Pettus, a Klan grand dragon, was elected by white Alabama to two terms in the United States Senate. Marchers in 1965 were beaten on a bridge bearing the name of the political tradition that had beaten their grandparents.

12.matchText: the Reconstruction narrative in this chain argues that its drafters made it deliberately narrow
12.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative argues the Fifteenth Amendment's drafters made it deliberately narrow, a negative prohibition on race-based denial rather than a positive guarantee of the ballot, and conspicuously silent on poll taxes and literacy tests. That silence was the loophole Jim Crow's disfranchisement machinery ran through for ninety-five years. The Voting Rights Act did not amend the amendment; it built the enforcement apparatus the 1870 Congress had refused to write.

13.matchText: the crop-lien system the Reconstruction narrative in this chain documents
13.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative's chapter on what replaced forty acres explains the crop-lien: a system in which debt rather than deeds tied the freedpeople to the plantation, an instrument with a race-neutral surface and a racially specific result. Redlining was the twentieth-century version, the federal government guaranteeing white mortgages and denying Black ones, the same machinery of race-specific wealth deprivation two generations apart.

13.matchText: in the 1919 Red Summer, white Chicago mobs had attacked Black neighborhoods
13.blurb: The Roaring Twenties narrative covers the 1919 Red Summer and the Chicago riot, when white mobs attacked Black neighborhoods for days after the Great Migration brought Black Southerners into the city. The Marquette Park mobs that shocked King in 1966 were the same phenomenon, white Northern resistance to residential integration, forty-seven years apart. King was not encountering something new in the North; he was encountering something the North had been doing the whole time.

15.matchText: a Senate select committee, the Church Committee, investigated and published it
15.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative's afterlife chapter sets up the longer pattern: the federal government that was finally forced to enforce the amendments was the same one that had abandoned them in 1877 and would tolerate, even run, campaigns against Black political power. COINTELPRO is the twentieth-century instance, the same Bureau that stood aside while the Freedom Riders were beaten running a clandestine, illegal operation to destroy the movement's most independent wing.

17.matchText: in the 1860s and 1870s Southern Black men voted nearly unanimously Republican, the party of Lincoln and emancipation
17.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative's Black Reconstruction chapter records the starting configuration exactly: Southern Black men voting nearly unanimously Republican, white Southerners nearly unanimously Democratic, Black-majority electorates putting Republican governments in Southern statehouses. The realignment after 1964 did not merely shift that arrangement; it inverted it almost term for term, the same two coalitions facing each other across the same line with only the party labels exchanged.

17.matchText: the 1919 Chicago riot, when white mobs attacked Black neighborhoods after the Great Migration brought Black Southerners north
17.blurb: The Roaring Twenties narrative documents the 1919 Chicago riot in its Red Summer chapter. Boston 1974, where mobs of thousands stoned buses carrying Black children, was the same phenomenon a half-century later: Northern white resistance to actual residential and school integration, a continuous, century-long pattern with no statute behind it to attack, which is exactly why the movement's Southern toolkit could never reach it.

18.matchText: the surviving correspondence has the Mississippi governor told in so many words that the North was
18.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative's Mississippi Plan chapter contains exactly this moment: in 1875 the besieged Republican governor Adelbert Ames asked Washington for troops to stop the open paramilitary campaign and was told, in writing, that the country was tired of these annual autumnal outbreaks. Mississippi was lost on purpose, by federal choice, two years before the formal Compromise of 1877. That refusal is the default the Second Reconstruction set out to reverse.

The Early American Republic narrative opens on the founding contradiction: a Declaration asserting all men are created equal written by and for a slaveholding republic. The founders knew it was a contradiction, said so in writing, and deferred it. This chapter closes the chain that began there: two and a half centuries spent on the distance between that sentence and what the republic actually did.

18.matchText: that the first Reconstruction tried and failed to close
18.blurb: The Reconstruction narrative's final chapter is the mirror of this one: the long afterlife of a deliberately destroyed revolution, the Lost Cause and the Dunning School smoothing the history almost before it was over, Du Bois demolishing the propaganda. The sanitizing of King's memory this chapter treats as an event is the same instinct, a generation later, that turned the cause of the Civil War into a myth: a country managing a painful history by sanding it smooth.

The Reconstruction narrative develops at length the argument that freedom without an economic base was freedom on paper only, and that the defeat of land redistribution, forty acres, doomed the freedpeople to sharecropping and debt. The Poor People's Campaign's economic bill of rights was refused in 1968 by, at bottom, the same mechanism: a governing coalition subordinating economic redistribution to propertied interests. The same promise was refused twice for the same reason.
